role_summary: SNCC field secretary; opened the Sunflower County voter-registration project in 1962; managed Fannie Lou Hamer's 1964 MFDP congressional campaign.
biographical_paragraph: In early August 1962, Charles McLaurin, twenty-one years old and out of Jackson, Mississippi, opened SNCC's voter-registration project in Sunflower County and led residents to the Indianola courthouse to try to register [src: 2]. He had come to that work through Medgar Evers, who turned him from the direct-action sit-ins of the Jackson Movement toward registration organizing [src: 2]. By the next month, night riders were firing into Black homes across the county in response [src: 2]. McLaurin worked Indianola and Ruleville alongside SNCC field secretaries Charlie Cobb and Landy McNair; in his own description, his organizing register was to "talk with the people, laugh with them, joke with them" [src: 2]. In 1964 he managed Fannie Lou Hamer's congressional campaign on the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party ticket, and he stayed close to Hamer on political and economic organizing through the 1970s [src: 2]. He later settled in Indianola, where he served as the city's Assistant Public Works Director until retirement [src: 2]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 17 of the Civil Rights History Project [src: 1].

sources.title: Charles McLaurin oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress
